coredata.dc:title: Design and preparation of a ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole for supercapacitor application: Importance and unique role of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiang
Wang, Jingmin
Liu, Jian
Wu, Ji
Chen, Hao
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: A ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole (GO/CDs/PPy) used as electrode active material for supercapacitor has been synthesized via in situ polymerization of GO/CDs with pyrrole under a mild condition. The CDs sandwiched between GO film and PPy layer can promote electron transportation in the ternary composite and thus reduce the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance of the electrode. Additionally, the CDs with large specific surface area can enhance the interfacial property among GO, CDs and PPy and increase the dielectric constant of the ternary composite. Notably, GO/CDs/PPy exhibits a specific capacitance of 576 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, and a supercapacitor fabricated by two symmetric GO/CDs/PPy electrodes owns a high energy density of 30.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 250 W kg−1. More importantly, the as–fabricated supercapacitor can keep an excellent cycle stability after 5000 cycles. In practice, a simple device assembled by five supercapacitors in series could power 59 light–emitting diode indicators for more than 60 s after only charging for 17 s. This work demonstrates that CDs can play an important and unique role in improving performance of a hybrid electric double layer capacitor (EDLC)/pseudocapacitor.

University of Science and Technology of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Science and Technology of China

Key Project of Natural Science Research of Universities in Anhui Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University Natural Science Research Project of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All of the co–authors thank Prof. Fapei Zhang (High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Science, Hefei 230031, P. R. China) for helping us to detect the electrical conductivity as well as the discussion provided by Dr. Lifeng Chen and Dr. Ziyou Yu (Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, P. R. China). Infrastructural supports from Georgia Southern University are sincerely acknowledged herein. This work was financed by the 211 project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 51272002), the Anhui Provincial Key Scientific and Technological Project (No. 1604a0902178), the Key Project of Natural Science Research of Universities in Anhui Province (No. KJ2016A577), and the 2016 Foundation for Imported Leading Talent Teams in Universities and Colleges of Anhui Province. The corresponding author (Pr. H. Bi) would sincerely thank Key Laboratory of Opto–electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation Ministry of Education, Anhui University and the Key Laboratory of Environmental–Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the synthesis process of GO/CDs/PPy composite. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (a) CDs (inset: the lateral size distribution) and (b) GO/CDs–2 (inset: HR–TEM image); SEM and TEM images of (c,d) GO/PPy and (e,f) GO/CDs/PPy–2 (inset: high–magnification TEM images). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorbance and PL emission (λ ex = 355 nm) spectra of the CDs dispersed in deionized water (inset: the photographs of CDs dispersed in deionized water); (b) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs, GO/CDs–2, CDs/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 dispersed in DMF; (c) FT–IR and (d) Raman spectra of CDs, GO, PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The electrochemical performances of PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 composites in a three–electrode system: (a) GCD curves at a current density of 0.5 A g−1; (b) GCD curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 at different current densities; (c) CV curves at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1; (d) CV curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 detected at various scan rates; (e) Preserved specific capacitances and coulombic efficiency of electrodes in 5000 charge–discharge cycles at a current density of 10.0 A g−1; (f) Nyquist plots in the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz (inset: the electrical equivalent circuit). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) I–V plots; (b) the real (ε′) and (c) the imaginary part of permittivities (ε′′) of PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2; (d) Typical Cole–Cole plots (ε′′ versus ε′) of GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The electrochemical performances of symmetric supercapacitors with PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 composites in a two–electrode system: (a) GCD curves at a current density of 0.5 A g−1; (b) GCD curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 at different current densities; (c) CV curves at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1; (d) CV curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 detected at various scan rates. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Ragone plots of the composites in this work and (b) Ragone plots of the GO/CDs/PPy–2 in this work vs. the composites in references; (c) a photograph of the assembled device (top) and a schematic illustration of the single symmetric supercapacitor (bottom); (d) a photograph of LED indicators lightened by the assembled device when the circuit was switched on. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Recently published data of graphene–based and GO–based electrode materials for supercapacitors in comparison with our work result of GO/CDs/PPy-2.

head.ce:title: Design and preparation of a ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole for supercapacitor application: Importance and unique role of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Georgia Southern University, 250 Forest Drive, Statesboro, GA, 30460, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Georgia Southern University, 75 Georgia Avenue, Statesboro, GA, 30460, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole (GO/CDs/PPy) used as electrode active material for supercapacitor has been synthesized via in situ polymerization of GO/CDs with pyrrole under a mild condition. The CDs sandwiched between GO film and PPy layer can promote electron transportation in the ternary composite and thus reduce the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance of the electrode. Additionally, the CDs with large specific surface area can enhance the interfacial property among GO, CDs and PPy and increase the dielectric constant of the ternary composite. Notably, GO/CDs/PPy exhibits a specific capacitance of 576 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, and a supercapacitor fabricated by two symmetric GO/CDs/PPy electrodes owns a high energy density of 30.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 250 W kg−1. More importantly, the as–fabricated supercapacitor can keep an excellent cycle stability after 5000 cycles. In practice, a simple device assembled by five supercapacitors in series could power 59 light–emitting diode indicators for more than 60 s after only charging for 17 s. This work demonstrates that CDs can play an important and unique role in improving performance of a hybrid electric double layer capacitor (EDLC)/pseudocapacitor.

A ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole (GO/CDs/PPy) shows a superior performance to PPy, CDs/PPy and GO/PPy when it was used as hybrid electrodes for a supercapacitor. The CDs sandwiched between GO and PPy play a key role in promoting electron transportation of the ternary composite system as well as enhancement of the interfacial property among GO, CDs and PPy.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most promising next generation energy–storage systems, supercapacitors have attracted extensive research attention due to their high power density and storage capacity, long cycle life, environmental friendliness, light weight, and small size. In general, supercapacitors can be classified into two categories with different energy–storage mechanisms: 1) electric double layer capacitors (EDLCs) and 2) pseudocapacitors. In the case of EDLCs, carbon–based materials, in particular graphene and graphene oxide (GO), are usually the preferred choices because of their good conductivity, high specific surface area and long cycle stability [1,2]. However, specific capacitances of EDLCs are limited due to the non–Faradaic charge storage mechanism. In contrast, pseudocapacitors show 10–100 times higher specific capacitances than the EDLCs due to the involvement of Faradaic charge storage mechanism, which is related to redox–reaction taken place in the bulk of active materials [3]. Transition-metal oxides and conductive polymers (CPs, e.g., polyaniline (PANI) and polypyrrole (PPy)) are commonly used active materials for pseudocapacitors, among which CPs are more promising for flexible supercapacitor applications. Unfortunately, CPs tend to swell and shrink during the charge/discharge processes, which leads to poor cycle stability that greatly restricts their practical utilizations [3,4]. Therefore, hybrid electrodes made of composites of carbon materials and CPs have been extensively studied to achieve enhanced capacitances and improved rate capabilities [3–13]. In recent years, various kinds of nanostructured PPy combining with graphene, GO or reduced GO (rGO) nanosheets have been prepared and applied to fabricate electrodes because PPy owns unique features such as high conductivity, fast charge–discharge mechanism, good thermal stability, low cost, and high energy density [6–9]. The fabrication methods mainly rely on the strong van der Waals attraction between highly aromatic graphene basal plane and π conjugated PPy chains [6]. Further, three–dimensional (3D) graphene/PPy as well as rGO/PPy hybrid aerogels were fabricated to increase effective surface area of the active materials, and achieve outstanding cycle stability with the specific capacitance up to 253 F g−1 and 473 F g−1, respectively [10,11]. More recently, a new 3D interconnected nanotubular graphene–PPy hybrid composite used for all–solid–state flexible supercapacitor filling with PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte shows a specific capacitance as high as 514 F g−1 at a discharge current density of 0.2 A g−1 [13].

Although the research on PPy–based hybrid supercapacitors have been progressed rapidly in the last several years, there are still some issues that remain to be overcome to achieve the device performances, like increase in both specific power and energy densities, developing eco–friendly electrolyte systems, and synthesis of electrode materials with desirable electrical/mechanical properties for intelligent and wearable devices [2,3,14,15]. In a hybrid EDLC/pseudocapacitor, the electrochemical charge storage ability of EDLC arises from the electronic and the ionic charge separations at the electrode–electrolyte interface. On the other hand, the Faradic charge transfer mechanism across the electrode–electrolyte interface significantly boosts the charge storage capacity. However, the realization of high specific capacitance is dependent on many factors such as material and structure of the electrodes, effective surface area of active materials, a fast ionic transport to the active sites, and the concentration of the adsorbed ions and amount of pseudocapacitance or Faradic charge transfer reactions.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new family of carbon nanoparticles with size below 10 nm, which usually consist of a nanocrystalline core with major sp2–hybridized carbon and an amorphous shell containing abundant surface functional groups [16–19]. They exhibit strong photoluminescence with high quantum yields as well as tunable band gaps, and thus usually called carbon quantum dots, in which graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are also included [16]. As a group of low–toxic, biocompatible and highly fluorescent quantum dots, CDs have shown tremendous potential for a wide range of chemical and biomedical applications [20–25]. Accompanied with excellent optical property, they are indeed good electron acceptors as well as electron donors [26,27]. Recently, GQDs//GQDs symmetric micro–supercapacitor with a high specific area capacitance (468.1 μF cm−2) was prepared by Yan's group [20]. More recently, the good electric conducting property of CDs were characterized by Bhattacharjee et al. [28]. Further, Nandi et al. found that in the GQDs–PANI hybrid, the dc conductivity increased by three orders compared with that of the GQDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs was drastically quenched in the GQDs–PANI hybrid, suggesting effective charge transfer between the GQDs and PANI chains [29]. Besides, fluorescence quenching in the CDs–GO system was also observed and attributed to the ultrafast electron transfer from CDs to GO with a time constant of 400 fs [30]. Although optoelectronic and photovoltaic properties of CDs doped CPs have been studied intensively so as to be efficient counter electrodes for dye–sensitized solar cells [29,31,32], unfortunately, the unique role of CDs in promoting the electrochemical behavior of CDs-containing polymeric composites has neither been explored, nor the possible contribution of CDs to the improvement of a hybrid EDLC/pseudocapacitor has been considered.

In the present work, a ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy is synthesized and used as electrode active material for supercapacitors. In this design, PPy will undertake faradaic reaction and act as electron donor, conductive GO can offer large surface area suitable for accepting electrons as well as collecting current [12,13]. It is expected that CDs sandwiched between PPy and GO can facilitate electron transportation through the active layer. Besides, CDs have larger specific surface area and will bring abundant interface into GO/CDs, CDs/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy composites, which can provide more surface area for access of electrolyte ions as well as enhanced multiple interfacial polarizations. Therefore, it is quite possible that the as–prepared GO/CDs/PPy composite achieves a higher electrochemical performance than binary composites of both CDs/PPy and GO/PPy, and becomes an alternative electrode material for flexible supercapacitors.

1,2–ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid (CA), ammonium persulfate (APS), H2SO4 (98 wt %), KMnO4, H2O2 (30 wt %), HCl (37 wt %), and graphite powder (GP) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. Pyrrole (Py) and camphorsulfonic acid (CSA) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Pyrrole was distilled before use, and other chemicals were used without further purification. All water used in this process is deionized water.

CDs was prepared by a simple one–step thermolysis method as shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) according to the previous literature [33]. Briefly, 5 mL of 1,2–ethylenediamine (EDA) was mixed thoroughly in 50 mL, 0.50 mol L−1 aqueous solution of citric acid (CA) after 20 min ultrasonication. Then, this mixture was heated for 5 min using a domestic microwave oven (800 W, Galanz, China) until the solution turned brown–yellow. After being cooled to room temperature naturally, the brown–yellow solution was filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for several times and then dialyzed (MWCO of 500) for 72 h in ultrapure water to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum conditions to obtain a brown powder product of CDs.

GO was synthesized from graphite powder by improved Hummer method [34–36]. Briefly, 0.5 g of graphite powder was mixed with 20 mL of H2SO4 (98 wt %) in a conical flask and stirred for 30 min in an ice–water bath. 0.5 g of NaNO3 and 3.0 g of KMnO4 were added slowly under continuous stirring. Then, the aforementioned solution was allowed to stir for two hours at room temperature. The solution was diluted with 40 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min 10 mL of H2O2 (30 wt %) and 100 mL of deionized water were added in turn to the above solution. The brownish yellow suspension was filtered and the filter cake was repeatedly washed with 0.5 mol L−1 HCl solution followed by deionized water to remove the residual sulfate ions. Lastly, GO was freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum conditions.

The GO/CDs composite samples were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. As shown in Figs. S2 and 40 mL of the aqueous CDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1) was added into 40 mL of the aqueous GO solution (5.0 mg mL−1) and treated under ultrasonic condition for 20 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor (100 mL capacity) and heated at 150 °C for 12 h in an oven. Finally, the black fluffy product was washed by deionized water and then freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum conditions. In order to adjust the mass content of CDs in the binary composite, we have prepared a series of binary composite samples of GO/CDs by feeding different mass ratios of CDs:GO (1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8, 1:10), and the resulting products were named as GO/CDs–1, GO/CDs–2, GO/CDs–3, GO/CDs–4, GO/CDs–5 and GO/CDs–6, respectively. As a control, the pure GO was also treated under a similar hydrothermal process except adding the CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1 , GO/CDs/PPy composite was fabricated via a modified in situ polymerization method [37,38]. In a typical synthesis, 0.1 g of GO/CDs was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water and treated under ultrasonic condition for 20 min in order to make a highly uniform dispersion. Next, the GO/CDs suspension was added to the mixture of Py and CSA (0.68 g of re–distilled Py and 0.46 g of CSA were added to 100 mL of deionized water) and mechanically stirred for 30 min at 500 rpm. The polymerization was initiated by adding a 100 mL of aqueous APS solution (0.01 mol L−1), and then continued at 4 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the solid products were separated by filtrating the solution through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Afterwards, the products were washed with deionized water for several times to remove reaction byproducts and un-reactants. The filter cake was freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum conditions. Actually we have synthesized a series of ternary composite samples of GO/CDs/PPy by using the same amounts of pyrrole (0.68 g) and GO/CDs (0.1 g) with different mass ratios of CDs:GO. According to the used GO/CDs samples (GO/CDs–1, GO/CDs–2, GO/CDs–3, GO/CDs–4, GO/CDs–5, GO/CDs–6), the as–synthesized ternary composites were named as GO/CDs/PPy–1, GO/CDs/PPy–2, GO/CDs/PPy–3, GO/CDs/PPy–4, GO/CDs/PPy–5, and GO/CDs/PPy–6, respectively. For comparison, samples of pure PPy, CDs/PPy, and GO/PPy were also polymerized under the same conditions.

The morphology was examined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM–2100) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, S–4800). The Fourier–transform infrared (FT–IR) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra of samples were recorded using a NEXUS–870 spectrometer and UV759 spectrophotometer, respectively. Powder X–ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on an X'Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) at a scanning rate of 2° min−1. The operation voltage and current were maintained at 36 kV and 20 mA, respectively. Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectroscope, equipped with a 532 nm laser. Elemental analysis data were collected by using a CHN Elementar (Vario EL cube, Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F–7000, Hitachi). For time–resolved fluorescence measurements, the fluorescence signals were collimated and focused onto the entrance slit of a monochromator with the output plane equipped with a photomultiplier tube (HORIBA FluoroMax–4). Thermo–gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a SDT Q600 by heating the samples at 10 °C min−1 from ambient temperature to 800 °C in N2 atmosphere. BET surface area of the powder samples were measured via N2 adsorption at −196 °C on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer. The real and imaginary part of permittivity (ε′, ε′′) of the samples in paraffin wax with the same weight concentration of 30% was measured by using an Agilent E5071 vector network analyzer in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz at room temperature.

All electrochemical experiments were carried out using 1 mol L−1 LiCl aqueous solution as electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests were recorded on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in three–electrode system. The working electrode was prepared by casting a nafion impregnated sample onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 4 mm. To avoid a reduction in sensitivity, GCE was carefully polished with 0.03 μm α–alumina powder and ultrasonically rinsed in deionized water at the end of each cycle. A platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were employed as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The potential windows of CV and GCD tests were set in the range from −0.2–0.8 V (vs. SCE). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was detected in a Zahner Zennium electrochemical workstation using an AC amplitude of 5 mV recorded at a frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz. The electrical conductivity measurements were performed on a probe station by a two–channel source meter (Keithley 2612A) at the room temperature. Meanwhile, a two–electrode cell system was used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of a supercapacitor by CV and GCD tests, and the potential windows were set in the range from 0 to 1 V. A symmetric supercapacitor was fabricated as follows: (1) the electrodes were composed of 80 wt% as–synthesized composite material, 10 wt% acetylene black and 10 wt% polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder; (2) a slurry consisting of the above mixture was smeared onto a piece of nickel sheet (2 × 2 cm2) with a loading of ca. 5.0 mg cm−2, dried at 60 °C in vacuum for 12 h and then compressed by ca. 10 MPa for 5 min; (3) both of electrodes were immersed into the electrolyte for 2 min, then taken out and assembled double electrode sandwich structure cell with a cellulose separator, then wrapped with parafilm (see Fig. S3).

Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image of CDs with a narrow size distribution histogram, which confirms that as–synthesized CDs are fairly uniform with an average diameter of 8.7 nm and a standard deviation of 1.4 nm. The size of GO sheet used in the present work is ca. 5–8 μm (see Fig. S4), which is commonly seen for the GO sheets obtained from the modified Hummer's method. As shown in Fig. S5, with feed mass ratios of CDs:GO changing from 1:10 to 1:8, 1:6, 1:4 and 1:2, the mass content of CDs in the binary composite samples of GO/CDs-6, GO/CDs-5, GO/CDs-4, GO/CDs-3 and GO/CDs-2 is increasing sequently. However, the mass content of CDs will not increase any more even though the feed mass ratio of CDs:GO has been promoted to 1:1, which means the maximum mass content of CDs that the binary composite of GO/CDs can hold (or cannot be washed away) is about 23.6 wt% by feeding in a mass ratio of CDs:GO = 1:2. Therefore, we have selected the binary composite of GO/CDs-2 with the feed mass ratio of CDs:GO (1:2) as an a typical sample for next synthesis and further study of ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy in this work.

Fig. 2(b) shows a representative TEM image of the GO/CDs–2, where CDs particles can be seen very clearly, decorating uniformly on the both sides of GO sheets. Furthermore, high–resolution TEM (HR–TEM) image (see the inset of Fig. 2(b)) reveals the CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.215 nm in the crystalline core corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [33,39]. As shown in Fig. S6, a strong characteristic peak of GO is observed at 11.0° in the XRD pattern of GO/CDs–2, However, during the hydrothermal synthesis of GO/CDs–2, the GO might be partially reduced to r-GO in the presence of CDs and hot water, which can be proved by the faint superimposed peaks from CDs and r-GO at ca. 22°. The SEM image in Fig. S7(a) shows a typical shape of PPy microspheres (ca. 0.25 μm in diameter), which does not change even after the incorporation of tiny CDs, as shown in Fig. S7(b). However, as for the GO/PPy sample, Fig. 2(c) shows a thick PPy layers have coated on both sides of the GO film, and no carbon dots is found from its high–magnification TEM image (Fig. 2(d)). In contrast, black CDs can be seen very clearly in the GO/CDs/PPy composite sample from its high–magnification TEM image as shown in Fig. 2(f). Besides, the ternary composite GO/CDs/PPy still keep the layered morphology probably due to the support of GO film (Fig. 2(e)). Notably, Fig. 2(b) shows the CDs decorating uniformly on the surface of the GO film in the GO/CDs composite before they are used for next-step synthesis of the ternary composite. Therefore, after in situ polymerization process, the CDs on the surface of GO film have been fully covered by PPy coating layer. That is to say, the CDs are sandwiched between the GO film and PPy layer in the ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy.

The UV–Vis absorbance and PL emission spectra of the CDs dispersed in deionized water are shown in Fig. 3 (a). UV–Vis absorption shows a narrow peak at 235 nm that are assigned to the π→π* transition of CDs [33], while the band at 355 nm is due to the n→π* transition of the functional groups present on the CDs [28]. The latter peak is prominent here due to the increased number of carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs by using CA as a raw material. When the aqueous solution of CDs is excited at 355 nm, a homologous PL emission peak appears at 450 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was estimated using the following equation (1) [40]:
(1)
φ =
φ r
×
( I /
I r
)
×
(
A r
/ A )
×
(
n 2
/
n r 2
)
where the φ is the QY, I is the integrated PL emission intensity (excited at 355 nm for CDs and quinine sulfate), n is the refractive index (1.334 for deionized water), A is the absorbance value (less than 0.1) of UV–Vis absorbance at 355 nm (deionized water), and the subscript ''r'' refers to the standard. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (φ r = 0.54) was selected as a standard control and the QY of CDs is calculated to be 38% (see Fig. S8 and Table S1). Fig. S9 shows the luminescence decay curves (excited at 355 nm) of CDs, which are quenched efficiently by either electron donor N,N–diethylaniline or electron acceptor 2,4–dinitrotoluene. Therefore, it can be determined that CDs are both excellent electron donors and electron acceptors [27]. In the present case, fluorescence decay times (Fig. 3(b)) of CDs, GO/CDs–2, CDs/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 dispersed in N,N–dimethylformamide (DMF) are recorded to be 6.5, 3.8, 1.1, and 0.8 ns, respectively. Obviously, the fluorescence decay time of CDs has shortened to the least in the ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy–2 in comparison with that in GO/CDs–2 or CDs/PPy, which reveals the co-existence of electron transfer between GO/CDs and between CDs/PPy in the same GO/CDs/PPy composite system.

The chemical structures of the GO, CDs, PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 were investigated by using FT–IR and Raman spectra (Fig. 3(c) and (d)). Several distinctive IR bands for GO can be observed at 1081, 1220, 1630, 1746, and 3420 cm−1 (see Table S2). However, characteristic peaks of CDs appear at 795, 1229, 1650, 1746, and 3305 cm−1, indicating abundant –COOH and –NH2 surface groups are present because CA and DEA were used as raw materials during the synthesis. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of PPy appear at 915, 1042, 1154, 1325, 1562, and 3240 cm−1 [41,42]. Obviously, the FT–IR spectrum of GO/CDs/PPy–2 contains major characteristic peaks of GO, CDs and PPy. Notably, the peak at 1154 cm−1 of PPy assigned to the stretching vibration of CN bond has shifted to 1162 and 1171 cm−1 in the spectra of GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2, respectively. And the peak at 1038 cm−1 representing in–plane vibration of =CH of Py ring has split and shifted to 1045 and 1065 cm−1, respectively. On the other hand, Fig. 3(d) shows two peaks located at 1352 and 1585 cm−1 in both Raman spectra of CDs and GO sheets, corresponding to the well–documented D and G bands of graphite, respectively [8,12]. The D/G band intensity ratio (I D/I G) expresses the atomic ratio of sp3/sp2–hybridized carbons, which is a measure of the extent of disordered graphite. The ratio of I D/I G in the GO spectrum is calculated to be 0.62, which is larger than that of CDs (0.42), probably because of abundant oxygen–containing groups on the surface of GO. PPy exhibits characteristic bands at 1572 and 1395 cm−1, which are associated with stretching of the π–conjugated structure and the ring stretching mode of PPy, however, both of bands vanish or fuse in the spectra of GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2 composites. Nevertheless, the presence of double peaks at about 1035 cm−1 and 920 cm−1, which are attributed to CH in–plane vibration and out–plane vibration of Py ring, reveals the existence of PPy in both GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2 composites [8]. Compared with those of PPy and GO/PPy, the decreased ratio of I D/I G (0.63) in the Raman spectrum of GO/CDs/PPy–2 suggests the presence of strong interactions among PPy, CDs and GO in this ternary composite due to the special doping effect of CDs.

Generally, the formation of GO/PPy hybrids can be driven by various interactions including the π–π interaction, hydrogen bonding, and the physical entanglement of the GO sheets [9,10,15,43]. In the present case, CDs have abundant oxygen– and nitrogen–containing surface groups, as observed from the FT–IR spectrum shown in Fig. 3(c). Zeta potential of CDs in water was measured to be −5.14 mV by using a Nano–ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd). Protonic acid such as CSA–doped PPy chain has positive charge in its N site and should interact well with the negative charged CDs. Thus, CDs attached to the surface of GO could act as nucleation sites for PPy growth on the GO surface during the in situ polymerization of composite of GO/CDs/PPy–2, resulting in many deformed PPy spheres with larger size (Fig. 2(e)) than that of the normal spherical PPy (see Fig. S7(a)). Seen from elemental analysis data as listed in Table S3, N element content in CDs is 16.82 wt%, which comes from EDA in microwave–assisted synthesis. As above–mentioned, FT–IR and Raman spectra (Fig. 3(c) and (d)) indicate that there are abundant –COOH and –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs. Besides, N content in PPy is 14.94 wt%, which almost comes from pyrrole rings. Although APS was used as an initiator and CSA was used as a doping acid during polymerization, the polymerized product was filtered and washed with deionized water repeatedly to remove reaction byproducts and reacting remnants. Further, no signals of APS and CAS can be found in XRD patterns of the as–prepared PPy and PPy–based composites. Notably, N–content in CDs/PPy is 15.19 wt%, which is a little smaller than that in N–doped CDs but a bit higher than that in PPy. Obviously, N–contents in both GO/PPy and GO/CDs–2 are less than that in PPy and CDs, respectively, which is due to very low N–content in GO. Therefore, it sounds reasonable that N–content in GO/CDs/PPy (14.05 wt%) is a little smaller than both in CDs/PPy and PPy, but a bit higher than that in GO/PPy. That is to say, N–content in the GO/CDs/PPy–2 has increased a little compared to that in the GO/PPy due to addition of highly N–doped CDs.

Furthermore, Fig. S10 shows TG curves of the as–prepared samples in temperature range of 20–800 °C. It can be seen that CDs has a ca. 34.1% weight loss in 100–600 °C due to removal of abundant –COOH and –NH2 surface groups. A steep weight loss near 185 °C in GO is attributed to the decomposition of unstable oxygen–containing groups on GO surface [44]. The residual oxygen–containing groups with high bond energy decompose slowly in the process of increasing temperature and a total weight loss of 51.2% reaches at 800 °C. However, a total weight loss of 39.5% for GO/CDs–2 indicates promotion of its thermal stability after combining GO with CDs. In the case of PPy, a total loss of 65.5 wt% can be achieved at 800 °C. Meanwhile, 59.5 wt % loss for GO/CDs/PPy–2, 60.9 wt% loss for CDs/PPy and 61.7 wt% loss for GO/PPy at 800 °C, indicating the sandwich–like structure of GO/CDs/PPy–2 has promoted its thermal stability. In addition, the mass percentages of PPy in CDs/PPy, GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2 are calculated from the weight loss of each TG curves [45]. As listed in Table S4, the mass percentages of PPy in CDs/PPy, GO/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy–2 to be 78.9%, 73.4% and 76.9%, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. S11, GCD curves were measured in a three–electrode system using 1 mol L−1 aqueous LiCl solution as electrolyte. The specific capacitances of active material on the single electrode (C s) can be calculated from the GCD curves using the following equation (2) [4,8–10,46]:
(2)
C s
= I × Δ t /
( m × Δ V )
where I, Δt, m and ΔV denote the discharge current, discharge time, the mass of active material on electrode and the potential window, respectively. According to Eq. (2), the sample of GO/CDs/PPy-2 exhibits a highest C s value of 576 F g−1, which is obviously higher than C s values of other composite samples such as GO/CDs/PPy-1 (561 F g−1), GO/CDs/PPy-3 (519 F g−1), GO/CDs/PPy-4 (502 F g−1), GO/CDs/PPy-5 (481 F g−1) and GO/CDs/PPy-6 (474 F g−1). Therefore, we have selected the sample of GO/CDs/PPy-2 with the feeding mass ratio of CDs:GO = 1:2 as an a typical example for further study and discussion in this paper. For comparison, the calculated C s value of PPy, CDs/PPy, and GO/PPy is 267, 305, and 468 F g−1, respectively.

GCD curves of GO/CDs/PPy-2 measured in different electrolyte solutions such as 1 mol L−1 LiCl,1 mol L−1 HCl, 1 mol L−1 H2SO4 or 1 mol L−1 KCl are shown in Fig. S12, which reveals that the capacitance value of the same electrode material may be varied if different electrolyte solutions are used. Table 1 has listed our work result and some other recently published data on graphene–based and GO–based electrode materials, including the major characteristics such as C s values, the electrolytes, and the used electrode system. It can be summarized that the capacitance behavior of GO/CDs/PPy–2 is better than those reported binary composites of graphene/PPy [10,13,38,44] and PPy/rGO [11] that were tested by using a similar three–electrode system, too. Furthermore, GCD curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 at different current densities are shown in Fig. 4(b). The C s values of GO/CDs/PPy–2 are calculated to be 576, 563, 545, 524, and 488 F g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10.0 A g−1, respectively. Thus, the ternary composite shows a lower specific capacitance with the increase of the current density, implying that the fast charging and discharging directly reduces the adsorption of electrolyte cations on the electrode.

Besides, the CV curves of the composites in a three–electrode system at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 are presented in Fig. 4(c). In general, an ideal EDLC shows a rectangular–shaped CV plot whereas the shape changes to a parallelogram for EDLCs with resistive losses. In Faradaic electrodes the electrical charge stored in the capacitor is strongly dependent on the potential, therefore, the voltammetry characteristics deviate from the parallelogram and redox peaks are observable for pseudocapacitors [2]. It can be seen from Fig. 4(c), influence of the pseudocapacitance on CV shape of GO/CDs/PPy–2 electrode is greater than that on GO/PPy, CDs/PPy and PPy electrodes, respectively. It confirms the presence of pseudocapacitance in the hybrid electrode made of GO/CDs/PPy–2 composites. However, there is no obvious redox peaks to be found as in a typical pseudocapacitor. This result suggests that the pseudocapacitance does not take a major role in this hybrid EDLC/pseudocapacitor made of GO/CDs/PPy–2 electrode material. However, it is noteworthy that GO/CDs/PPy–2 has almost the same specific surface area as that of CDs/PPy-2 (Table S5), but its C s (576 F g−1) is almost two times of the latter (305 F g−1) while measured at the same current density. This fact reveals that there are some other major effects accounting for the enhanced supercapacitance besides pseudocapacitance. In addition, CV curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 at various scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1 (Fig. 4(d)) have demonstrated a good and stable capacitive behavior under the condition of 1.0 V operating voltage.

The composites were cycled for 5000 times at a current density of 10.0 A g−1 to estimate their cycle stability (see Fig. 4(e)). After 1000 charge–discharge cycles, the capacitance retention of PPy (82.9%) became a bit lower than those of CDs/PPy (85.7%), GO/PPy (93.1%) and GO/CDs/PPy–2 (97.0%). In comparison with the low capacitance retention of PPy (66.9%), CDs/PPy (72.4%) and GO/PPy (83.7%) after 5000 cycles, the cycle stability of GO/CDs/PPy–2 has been significantly improved with a higher capacitance retention of 92.9%. Besides the high mechanical strength and electrochemical stability of GO, the large interface at GO and PPy as well as an enhanced interfacial property by the CDs incorporated between GO and PPy might have a more important effect on hindering the swelling and shrinkage of PPy during the repeating charge–discharge processes.

In addition, coulombic efficiency of electrode is an important indicator for supercapacitor and can be calculated from the electric charge ratio of the GCD process [47,48]. As shown in Fig. 4(e), with the increasing cycles, the coulombic efficiency of GO/CDs/PPy–2 has increased from 88% to 99% after 17 cycles at a current density of 10.0 A g−1 and subsequently remains at 99% over 5000 cycles. As a control, the coulombic efficiencies of GO/PPy, CDs/PPy and PPy have merely increased from 73% to 97% after 77 cycles, 64%–92% after 355 cycles and 56%–90% after 1095 cycles, respectively. Moreover, the GCD curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 become linear lines after 5000 cycles (see Fig. S13), which provides an evidence that GO/CDs/PPy–2 with 1 mol L−1 aqueous LiCl solution is stable under charge–discharge cycles and exhibits an excellent capacitance behavior. Hence, the high coulombic efficiency as well as an excellent cycle stability reveals the high quality and uniformity of the GO/CDs/PPy–2 electrode in this system [49].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis has been recognized as one of the principal methods examining the fundamental behavior of electrode materials for supercapacitors [10,50]. The resulting Nyquist plots (Fig. 4(f)) consist of a semicircle arc at the high frequency region and a straight line at the low frequency region, and then the measured impedance spectra were analyzed by fitting the impedance data with the equivalent circuit (the inset of Fig. 4(f)). The relevant equivalent electrical circuit consists of an equivalent serial resistance (R s), connected in series with a parallel combination of an electrochemical charge transfer resistance (R ct) and a Wurburg resistance (Z W) with a double–layer capacitance (C dl). The first intersection with the real axis at high–frequencies region is a combination of the electrolytic resistance, the internal resistance of the composite electrode, and the contact resistance at the interface between active material and current collector [9,38]. The semicircle appearing at high-frequency region is attributed to the R ct caused by the Faradic reactions and the double–layer capacitance on the grain surface. The 45° slope portion of the curve is the Wurburg resistance (Z W) which is a result of the frequency dependence of ionic diffusion/transport in the electrolyte and to the surface of the electrode. At low frequencies, the impedance plot should theoretically be a vertical line, which is parallel to the imaginary axis. In fact, the low frequency straight line always departs from that expected with a slope angle close to 90° due to the existence of “constant phase element”. The nearly vertical line indicate a pure capacitive behavior and low diffusion resistance of ions in the structure of the electrodes [51]. The R s values of GO/CDs/PPy–2, GO/PPy, CDs/PPy, and PPy were estimated to be 0.73, 3.51, 0.81, and 0.86, respectively. As the same electrolyte and current collector were used in all EIS measurements, which means equal solution–phase resistance in all cases. The GO/PPy presents a higher R s than PPy due to the poor electrical conductivity of GO. Obviously, the conductivity of composites was enhanced because of the incorporation of CDs in composites. This result is consistent with the data of electric conductivity later (see next paragraph). Moreover, the R ct values for the GO/CDs/PPy–2, GO/PPy, CDs/PPy, and PPy electrodes are 1.42, 2.39, 8.61, and 9.92, respectively. The Z W values of GO/CDs/PPy–2, GO/PPy, CDs/PPy, and PPy electrodes are 0.09, 0.11, 0.38, and 0.45, respectively. As for GO/CDs/PPy–2, the semicircle with a smallest diameter can be observed at high–frequency region, indicating a higher charge transfer rate between the electrode material and electrolyte due to CDs–assisted enhancement of electron transportation from PPy to GO with the synergistic effect among PPy, GO and CDs. The slope of straight line at low frequency region for GO/CDs/PPy–2 electrode is bigger than others, indicating a nearly ideal capacitive behavior of GO/CDs/PPy–2 electrode material.

In this work, CDs have two main effects on the property of ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy, which makes them play an important and unique role in enhancement of electrochemical performance for supercapacitor application. The two main effects are addressed as follows:
(1)
The incorporation of conductive CDs can promote the electron transportation in all of the CDs-containing composite materials such as GO/CDs, CDs/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy. It is well-known that electrical conductivity of GO is very poor due to the presence of highly electrical resistant groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or epoxy in GO sheets. However, we have measured the electrical conductivities of the series of binary composite samples of GO/CDs-1, GO/CDs-2, GO/CDs-3, GO/CDs-4, GO/CDs-5 and GO/CDs-6 using a probe station by a two–channel source meter (Keithley 2612A) at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S14(a), all of the electrical conductivities of the six GO/CDs composite samples are superior to that of GO. The higher mass content of CDs in the GO/CDs composite, the higher electrical conductivity of the GO/CDs composite can achieve. Notably, the electrical conductivity of GO/CDs–2 (3.66 × 10−2 S cm−1) is 50-fold greater than that of GO (6.12 × 10−4 S cm−1) when the mass content of CDs increased to the maximum of 23.6 wt% in GO/CDs composite. Moreover, we have measured the electrical conductivities of the series of ternary composite samples of GO/CDs/PPy-1, GO/CDs/PPy-2, GO/CDs/PPy-3, GO/CDs/PPy-4, GO/CDs/PPy-5 and GO/CDs/PPy-6 in the same method, too. Fig. S14(b) shows that the conductivity of GO/CDs/PPy series is also increasing with the increase of CDs mass content in the ternary composites, in particular, GO/CDs/PPy–2 exhibits a conductor-like behavior with a highest conductivity of 1.97 S cm−1. For comparison, we have also measured electrical conductivities of the PPy, CDs/PPy, and GO/PPy in the same way. Fig. 5 (a) has demonstrated very clearly that in our work, CSA (a protonic acid)–doped PPy shows a typical conducting polymer behavior with an electrical conductivity of 0.86 S cm−1, however, the electrical conductivity of CDs/PPy is obviously promoted to 1.24 S cm−1 while incorporation of CDs into PPy. On the contrary, the conductivity of GO/PPy is decreased to 0.25 S cm−1 because of addition of the GO with a poor conductivity. It is noteworthy that the electrical conductivity of ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy can be much more enhanced than those of CDs-containing binary composites such as GO/CDs and CDs/PPy. Besides, Fig. 4(f) shows both the internal resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode materials made of GO/CDs/PPy can be reduced for comparison to those made of pure PPy, GO/PPy, or CDs/PPy. All of the results have demonstrated that the conductive CDs sandwiched between GO and PPy can facilitate electron transportation from PPy to GO in the ternary composite system of GO/CDs/PPy.
(2)
The addition of CDs can increase the dielectric property of the ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy. Fig. 5(b) and (c) show ε′ and ε′′ dependency of GO/CDs/PPy, GO/PPy, CDs/PPy and PPy in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz. Generally, ε′ and ε′′ are the real and imaginary part of permittivity, representing dielectric constant and dielectric loss of material, respectively. Upon incident electromagnetic wave, dielectric polarization in the absorbing material may occur, including dipole polarization, interfacial polarization and electron/ion displacement polarization [52,53]. Generally, dipole polarization take place only at low frequency range (<1 GHz) while electron displacement polarization will happen in high frequency range just because the effective mass of an electron is so small that it can follow the fast frequency change immediately in the microwave range [54]. As shown in Fig. 5(d), both the real part and imaginary part of permittivity of GO/CDs/PPy–2 have increased in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz compared with those of GO/PPy. As for GO/CDs/PPy–2 composite system, multi–interfacial polarization might occur due to high specific surface area of carbon dots in an average size below 10 nm and large interface area between CDs and PPy. Moreover, as above-mentioned, the conductive CDs sandwiched between GO and PPy can facilitate electron transportation from PPy to GO in the ternary composite system of GO/CDs/PPy, and thus electron displacement polarization will be greatly enhanced.

In this work, a hybrid electrode comprised of EDL and pseudocapacitor has been designed and fabricated. Moreover, the capacitance of a typical EDLC, as charge accumulated at the electrode–electrolyte interface, is generally defined by equation (3) [2]:
(3)
C =
ε 0
ε r
A / D
where ε 0 is the permittivity of free space, ε r is the dielectric constant of an electrolyte, A is the specific surface area of the electrode, and D is the distance between the two electrodes. BET surface area of PPy, CDs/PPy, GO/CDs–2, GO/PPy, and GO/CDs/PPy–2 powders is listed in Table S5. Although the incorporation of CDs can make the specific surface area of GO/CDs/PPy–2 (21.84 m2 g−1) a bit larger than those of PPy (8.864 m2 g−1) and GO/PPy (7.615 m2 g−1), but almost equal to that of CDs/PPy (19.39 m2 g−1). The modest increase is not satisfied, probably because both sides of GO film decorated with CDs have been covered by a thick layer of PPy, as shown in Fig. 2(e).

Although the impact of the increased dielectric property of GO/CDs/PPy material on the supercapacitor made of the GO/CDs/PPy hybrid electrodes has not been fully understood yet, the increased multi–interfacial polarization as well as the enhanced electron displacement polarization may contribute to the capability of storing charges and thus a remarkable increase of specific capacitance in GO/CDs/PPy–2 (see Fig. 4(a)).

Furthermore, symmetric supercapacitors were assembled by these electrode materials, and a two–electrode cell configuration was used to evaluate the capacitance performance [46,55–58]. The GCD curves are shown in Fig. 6 (a), and the specific capacitances of the assembled supercapacitors (C cell) have been estimated using the following equation (4) [9,46]:
(4)
C
cell
= I × Δ t /
(
m
total
× Δ V )
where I, Δt, m total and ΔV denote the discharge current, discharge time, the total mass of active material on two electrodes, and the potential window, respectively. That is, at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the C cell of GO/CDs/PPy–2 (216.8 F g−1) is superior to those of PPy (81.9 F g−1), CDs/PPy (97.8 F g−1), and GO/PPy (170.9 F g−1). As for GO/CDs/PPy–2, C cell of the fabricated symmetric supercapacitor are assessed as 216.8, 210.7, 202.3, 189.6, and 172.5 F g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10.0 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). Normally, the electrochemical properties of electrode materials for supercapacitors can be studied in either a three–electrode or a two–electrode system. Although the capacitance measured in a three–electrode system is generally superior to that estimated in a two–electrode cell configurations, the two–electrode system is believed to approach more closely to the real applications of supercapacitors [59]. Moreover, the CV curves present typical capacitive behaviours with quasi–rectangular profiles (Fig. 6(c)). Clearly, the CV curve of the ternary composite shows a bigger rectangle in shape compared with others at the same scan rate of 10 mV s−1, indicating a better electrochemical performance [13]. Fig. 6(d) exhibits the CV curves of GO/CDs/PPy–2 at scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1, respectively. All of the nearly rectangular CV curves indicate an ideal capacitive behavior of the assembled device with the ternary composite.

The Ragone plots (see Fig. 7 (a)) of the assembled supercapacitors can be calculated from galvanostatic discharge data, in which the specific energy density (E m) and power density (P m) of the capacitor can be calculated according to the following equations (5) and (6) [5,43,46,60]:
(5)
E m
= 1 / 2 ×
C
cell
× Δ
V 2
(6)
P m
=
E m
/ Δ t
where C cell, ΔV and Δt denote the specific capacitance of an assembled supercapacitor calculated from Eq. (4), the potential window and the discharge time, respectively. From Eq. (5) and Eq. (6), the maximum E m of GO/CDs/PPy–2 is 30.1 Wh kg−1 while the P m achieves 250 W kg−1. Moreover, it can still preserve 79.5% of the E m as the P m increases to 5000 W kg−1. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the obtained maximum E m in this work is higher than some of recently reported graphene/GO–based or PPy–based electrode materials that possessing the same power density, for examples, graphene/SnO2/PPy (19.4 Wh kg−1) [37], PPy/MGO (8.49 Wh kg−1) [9], GO/PPy (16.4 Wh kg−1) [43], V2O5/PPy (14.2 Wh kg−1) [61], PPy/GR (8.77 Wh kg−1) [62], rGO/PPy (5.7 Wh kg−1) [63] and graphene/PPy (10.5 Wh kg−1) [64].

To further assess the actual application value of the prepared ternary composite, a small device was assembled by connecting five symmetric supercapacitors in series, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The assembled device was charged for 17 s to 5.0 V at a current density of 10.0 A g−1, then, 59 light–emitting diode (LED, 3 mm diameter) indicators can be lightened by the assembled device (see Fig. 7(d)) and most of the LED indicators were continuously illuminated for more than 60 s (see Fig. S15).

In summary, a ternary composite of GO/CDs/PPy–2 with an excellent electrochemical performance has been synthesized. The hybrid electrodes made of GO/CDs/PPy–2 composite not only show a larger specific capacitance and higher energy density than those of PPy, CDs/PPy and GO/PPy, but also possess excellent capacitance retention after 5000 cycles. These results have demonstrated that CDs can play an active role in improving performance of a hybrid EDLC/pseudocapacitor owing to the following main reasons: (1) One main reason is that the conductive CDs sandwiched between GO and PPy can facilitate electron transportation from PPy to GO in the ternary composite system, and hence reduce both the internal resistance (R s) and charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the electrode materials. (2) The other reason relies on the fact that ultra-small sized CDs have larger specific surface area and will bring abundant interfaces into GO/CDs, CDs/PPy and GO/CDs/PPy composites, which can provide more surface area for access of electrolyte ions as well as enhanced multiple interfacial polarizations. The greatly enhanced interfacial property can not only increase the dielectric constant of the composite material and thus the capacitance, but also overcome the drawback of the poor cycle stability of those conducting polymer-based composite system when applied for supercapacitors. Above all, the advantages induced by incorporation of CDs into the hybrid electrode materials encourage us to search for a new pathway to fabricate high–performance, flexible and stable supercapacitors in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All of the co–authors thank Prof. Fapei Zhang (High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Science, Hefei 230031, P. R. China) for helping us to detect the electrical conductivity as well as the discussion provided by Dr. Lifeng Chen and Dr. Ziyou Yu (Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, P. R. China). Infrastructural supports from Georgia Southern University are sincerely acknowledged herein. This work was financed by the 211 project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 51272002), the Anhui Provincial Key Scientific and Technological Project (No. 1604a0902178), the Key Project of Natural Science Research of Universities in Anhui Province (No. KJ2016A577), and the 2016 Foundation for Imported Leading Talent Teams in Universities and Colleges of Anhui Province. The corresponding author (Pr. H. Bi) would sincerely thank Key Laboratory of Opto–electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation Ministry of Education, Anhui University and the Key Laboratory of Environmental–Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.01.005.

Investigations of poly(pyrrole)–coated cotton fabrics prepared in blends of anionic and cationic surfactants as flexible electrode

Enhanced interfacial polarization relaxation effect on microwave absorption properties of submicron–sized hollow Fe3O4 hemispheres
Broadband microwave absorption of CoNi@C nanocapsules enhanced by dual dielectric relaxation and multiple magnetic resonances

Enhanced capacitance and rate capability of graphene/polypyrrole composite as electrode material for supercapacitors
